title: When the Body Remembers: Unseen Wounds of Religious Trauma
dek: Religious trauma is not only an emotional burden but a somatic reality, etched deeply into the body’s muscles like the jaw and psoas. Sacred spaces meant for sanctuary can paradoxically become sources of betrayal, where the body silently holds memories of shame, submission, and suppressed fury long after the mind tries to forget.
bodyMarkdown:
## Introduction: When the Pews Crumble Beneath You

It’s 10:37 on a Sunday morning, and you’re sitting in a wooden pew that smells faintly of old varnish and forgotten hymnals. The church is quiet but for the occasional creak of settling timbers and the faint rustle of a choir robe. Your skin is cool against the polished wood, but inside your body, there’s a low-burning fire you dare not name aloud. Your jaw clenches so tightly the masseter muscle hums with tension, a clenched fortress guarding a secret wound. Your diaphragm tightens, shallow breaths barely stirring the ribs. The betrayal—the deep, aching rupture of a place that promised sanctuary but delivered shame and silence—resides in every taut fiber of your body.

This is the somatic landscape of religious trauma: the invisible map etched into your fascia and muscles by sacred spaces that demanded submission and punished fury. This is not just emotional memory but biological reality. As Bessel van der Kolk teaches us, trauma lives in the body—it keeps the score. The church, an institution built on faith and community, can also become a crucible of betrayal that your body remembers long after your mind has tried to forget.

## The Body Remembers: Jaw, Psoas, and the Weight of Betrayal

You might not realize how your body is speaking when you sit through a service that echoes with the ghosts of past wounds. The masseter muscle, that thick band along your jawline, clenches in a silent scream. It’s the ultimate lockdown, a way your body physically braces against the intolerable: the sermons that demanded obedience, the whispered judgments that silenced your rage, the altar that required you to swallow your fury as if it were poison.

Below the surface, the psoas—a deep core muscle that connects your lower spine to your femur—tightens like a coiled spring. This muscle is the body’s ancient sentinel, responsible for the fight-or-flight response. When the church, a supposed sanctuary, becomes a place of spiritual threat, the psoas doesn’t forget. It hardens, constricting your ability to feel safe in your own body, anchoring the betrayal in your very core. You might notice the stiffness when you rise from the pew, a subtle but unyielding resistance in your hips and lower back. This is not a glitch in your body but a biological record of being told your truth was dangerous.

Van der Kolk’s work reminds us that trauma isn’t a story we can simply “get over.” It is stored in the neural pathways and musculature, in the way your diaphragm clamps down so you barely breathe during a prayer that feels like a command to disappear. The church’s demand for submission becomes a physical architecture inside you, a fortress that isolates your body from its own rage and grief.

## Sitting Still While Your Body Screams

You might think sitting still in a pew is passive, but inside your body, it’s a battle. The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles, spanning your neck and shoulders, tighten as if bracing for impact. The weight of expectation—the preached perfectionism, the unyielding moral codes—presses down like an invisible hand on your clavicles. Your breath becomes shallow, the diaphragm reluctant to expand, as if fear itself is pressing on your lungs.

This somatic experience is not just metaphorical. It is real and raw. Your body is screaming in tension and tightness, in muscle knots and shallow breath, in the subtle tremors that betray your stillness. The church’s betrayal is written in the way your jaw clenches so hard it aches, in the way your psoas seizes up so your hips and lower back feel perpetually on edge. This is not a failing of will or character; it is biology responding to spiritual and institutional trauma.

Teachers like Karla McLaren and Resmaa Menakem have shown us that the body carries the emotional residue of betrayal, especially in spaces that demanded you hide your fury. The church that wounded you might have taught you that anger was ugly, sinful, or dangerous. But your body knows better. It holds your rage in the masseter, your fear in the psoas, and your sorrow in the diaphragm. To honor this is to begin a somatic conversation with the parts of you that still remember sitting in that pew, trembling and clenched, beneath a roof that promised salvation but delivered silence.

This is not about pathology. This is biology—your body’s fierce, unyielding record of what happened when your spirit was cornered and your fury shamed. And it’s the first step toward reclaiming your body’s voice, your anger’s language, and your own sacred sanctuary.

## When Control Becomes the Cage: Feeling the Anger Ignite

You sit with it now—the sudden flare of heat rising behind your sternum, low and insistent like embers catching dry wood. This isn’t the polite anger you were taught to hide, the quick flush you could swallow down with a prayer or a whispered “forgive them.” No, this is the raw, searing fire that demands recognition. It radiates outward, pressing hot against your ribs, an unmistakable signal that something vital has been violated.

The church, which once promised sanctuary, instead became a cage. The teachings—holy words meant to guide—were wielded as shackles, binding your voice, your body, your very spirit. That tightening at the trapezius muscle, the one that runs along the base of your neck and across your shoulders? It’s the body’s memory of carrying the weight of imposed silence, the unyielding pressure to conform. You can feel it knotting now, a physical echo of the emotional constriction you endured.

This anger is not just a feeling; it’s a message from your body, a boundary alarm blaring through your fascia and muscle fibers. Karla McLaren reminds us that anger is fundamentally a boundary-restoring emotion. When someone—an institution, a person, a belief system—pushes past your limits, your anger rises to draw the line. It’s a fierce guardian, not a villain. Your trapezius clamps down as if to hold your shoulders back, as if to remind you to stand tall even when you’ve been told to bow. The masseter, that muscle of the jaw, clenches tightly, holding back words you were never allowed to speak.

## Releasing the Fire: The Body’s Somatic Wisdom

Here’s where the body insists on being heard: the heat in your chest is an invitation to move, to release. It’s 3 a.m. and the night is thick with the scent of damp earth through your cracked window. You sit up, your diaphragm locked tight beneath the weight of your ribs, and let a slow, deliberate breath draw the fire outward. Each exhale loosens the tension in your trapezius, the knot that’s been there since you first learned that anger was sinful.

You might rock gently back and forth, or press your fists into the floor, anchoring yourself as the rage pulses through your veins. The levator scapulae, those muscles that lift your shoulder blades, tremble under the strain of holding back for so long. Now, they begin to soften, releasing the old patterns of suppression. The body’s fascia, that intricate web of connective tissue, remembers the liberation that comes with expression, the flood of oxygen and blood that follows a real release.

This isn’t catharsis as a quick fix, but a somatic recalibration—a slow, steady uncoiling of the body’s stored defenses. You’re not just feeling anger; you’re embodying it, letting it speak through your body’s language. Pema Chödrön says to welcome difficult emotions with curiosity and kindness, and this is what you do. You listen to the roar in your chest without judgment, knowing this is biology, not pathology.

## Herbal Allies for Nervous System Repair

The nervous system, overwhelmed by the chronic dissonance between your inner truth and external control, needs gentle support. This is a dysregulation born of trauma—not the dramatic, headline-grabbing kind, but the slow drip of spiritual betrayal that leaves your autonomic nervous system stuck in a loop of fight, flight, or freeze. The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles tighten as part of this locked state, holding your body in a state of hypervigilance.

Herbal nervines and adaptogens become your allies here, medicines that honor the body’s need to regain balance without dulling the fierce clarity of your anger. Herbs like skullcap and oatstraw soothe the nervous system’s frayed edges, softening the tight bands of tension while supporting resilience. [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) is particularly potent for releasing the jaw’s clenched grip and calming the racing heart that often accompanies the surfacing of deeply held anger.

Adaptogens such as ashwagandha and rhodiola teach the adrenal glands to modulate the stress response, allowing the nervous system to find its equilibrium again. They don’t erase the fire; rather, they temper it so you can wield your anger with precision and clarity, not chaos. This is crucial—your anger is a boundary signal, not a blight. Supporting your nervous system with these herbs gives your body the tools to say “enough” without shutting down or exploding.

You might brew a tea of skullcap and oatstraw in the quiet hours before dawn, the steam carrying the scent of fresh earth and green leaves. As you sip, the heat in your chest begins to shift from a wildfire to a steady ember, the trapezius muscles slowly unwinding like a tightly coiled spring. This ritual becomes a reclamation of your body’s wisdom, a way to honor the anger that rose when control was mistaken for care.

The church’s teachings may have tried to mute your fury, but your body remembers. The muscles remember. The fascia remembers. And now, with the help of ancestral plant medicine and the somatic wisdom of your own flesh, you can respond—not with shame, but with the fierce grace of a boundary restored.

## Moving Anger Through the Diaphragm and Vocal Cords

You carry this anger in your body, not just in your mind. It’s a tightness in the diaphragm—the dome-shaped muscle that separates your chest from your belly. When you were silenced, shamed, or betrayed by the church, that muscle contracted, pulling your breath short, locking your voice in. The diaphragm hardens like a clenched fist inside your ribcage, the same way your jaw’s masseter muscles tighten when you’re trying not to speak your truth. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body remembers the moment you were told your fury was ugly, unholy, or worse, sinful.

The vocal cords—those delicate bands of muscle in your throat—are often constricted by years of holding back your rage. You might notice a raspiness when you finally let out a yell, or a rawness after speaking your anger aloud. That rawness is your body’s way of waking up, of reclaiming the breath and sound that were stolen. The levator scapulae, those muscles running from your neck to your shoulders, may ache with the effort of holding your head down, your voice down. The trapezius muscles, too, bear the weight of this silent fury.

To integrate this anger, you must move it through these muscles and fascia, through the diaphragm and vocal cords, so it no longer festers under your skin. This is not about screaming into the void or lashing out at others. It is about reclaiming the sound of your body’s truth, the rawness of your breath, and the freedom of your voice.

## An Embodied Practice: Breath and Voice Release

It is 6:47 a.m., the sky still bruised with the blue-grey of early dawn. You sit on a firm cushion on the floor, your feet grounded, your spine erect but relaxed. The air smells faintly of damp earth and the dried sage you burned last night to clear the stale energy from your bedroom. Inhale slowly through your nose, filling your belly first, then your ribs, and finally your upper chest. Feel the diaphragm expand downward, pressing lightly against your abdominal organs. Hold this breath for a count of four.

Now, tighten your pelvic floor and engage your transverse abdominis—a corset muscle wrapping around your waistline—holding the tension as you prepare to exhale. On the exhale, release the breath forcefully through your open mouth, allowing a low, guttural sound to emerge. It might start as a hum, a growl, or a long "ahhh" that vibrates in your chest and throat. As you exhale, consciously release the tension in your masseter, the muscles clenching your jaw, and the levator scapulae along your neck. Let the trapezius muscles drop away from your ears.

Repeat this cycle five times, each exhale longer and louder than the last, until the sound moves from the back of your throat to the front of your mouth, becoming clearer and freer. Notice the warmth spreading in your chest, the pulse behind your eyes, the ease in your shoulders. The body is releasing what language couldn’t hold.

Once you complete the breath and voice release, rest your hands on your belly and breathe gently for a few minutes. Feel the slow rise and fall of your diaphragm, the softness returning to your throat. The body knows how to heal when you give it room to move, to sound, to express.

## FAQ: Common Fears About Leaving the Church and Feeling Your Anger

**Q: What if I feel like I’m betraying my family or community by leaving the church?**  
A: This fear lives in your psoas muscle, that deep core muscle that tenses when you feel threatened or torn. It’s your body telling you that this is a hard choice, that you’re stepping into the unknown. But understand: loyalty to yourself is not betrayal. The church’s teachings may have asked you to sacrifice your anger, your boundaries, your very self for the sake of belonging. Your body remembers what that cost. Leaving is an act of survival and self-respect. You don’t have to sever every connection immediately, but honor the signals in your body guiding you toward safety.

**Q: I’m afraid that feeling this anger will make me a bad person or lose my faith entirely.**  
A: This is a common story embedded in many religious teachings—that anger is sinful or makes you less worthy. But anger is a messenger, not a moral judgment. It’s stored in your diaphragm when you don’t express it, in your vocal cords when you silence yourself. Feeling it fully, somatically, doesn’t make you bad; it makes you human. The anger often points to a deeper grief or betrayal that your faith tradition never allowed you to name. You can hold this anger and still find your own kind of faith—or simply be a spiritual being with boundaries and self-compassion.

**Q: How do I know if my anger is safe to express? What if I hurt someone?**  
A: Safety starts in your body. When the trapezius and levator scapulae ease, when your breath flows freely through the diaphragm and vocal cords without shaking or going into a panic, you are more likely to express anger without harm. This is why somatic practices are essential—they teach you to feel your edges, to release tension before it becomes rage. You can create boundaries and say “no” firmly without yelling or violence. Anger expressed with awareness is a protector, not a weapon.

**Q: Can herbs help me with the physical tension of this anger?**  
A: Absolutely. Herbs like skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) and passionflower (Passiflora incarnata) support the nervous system, helping soothe the diaphragm and reduce muscular tension in the neck and shoulders. Licorice root (Glycyrrhiza glabra) can support adrenal health when anger is chronic and exhausting. You might try a tincture or tea in the early evening, when your diaphragm often tightens and your jaw clenches after a long day of holding back. Remember, this is not a quick fix but a slow medicine, grounding your body as you work with your anger.

**Q: What if I can’t find a therapist who understands this kind of anger?**  
A: Many therapists are trained to pathologize anger or urge you to “calm down” as if your body’s alarm is a flaw. Look for somatic therapists, bodyworkers, or guides who center the body-first approach—teachers like Resmaa Menakem or Bessel van der Kolk emphasize the wisdom of the nervous system, not just the mind. If you cannot find one locally, consider online sessions or workshops with these guides. You are not alone in this, even if it often feels that way.

---

The body remembers. The jaw remembers. The diaphragm remembers. This is not sin or failure; this is biology. Your anger is a message, a force that demands recognition, that asks to be moved through your breath and voice. When you let it pass, you reclaim the sound that the church tried to silence. You are not ugly. You are not broken. You are fiercely, wildly alive.
